coredata.dc:title: Red-to-blue colorimetric probe based on biomass carbon dots for smartphone-integrated optosensing of Cu(II) and L-cysteine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dianwei
Zhang, Furui
Wang, Shengnan
Hu, Sha
Liao, Yonghong
Wang, Fenghuan
Liu, Huilin
coredata.dc:description: We constructed a smartphone-integrated optosensor with inexpensive, reversible, environmental friendly, and rapid adsorption to detect Cu(II) and L-cysteine (L-Cys). The key part of this study was to prepare a red-to-blue colorimetric probe from herbaceous andrographis paniculata using one-pot polymerization at room temperature. When Cu(II) existed, the red fluorescence on the surface of the core–shell probe was quenched, while the blue fluorescence of the core did not respond, because the colorimetric probe interacted with the Cu(II) on the surface of red CDs. After L-Cys added, it interacted with the Cu(II) to strip it from the surface of red CDs, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence response. Under optimal conditions, the detection limits of this method for Cu(II) and L-Cys were 71 nM and 12 nM, respectively. Further, the red-to-blue colorimetric probe was integrated into smartphone with a software application to convert fluorescent color images into specific red (R), green (G), and blue (B) values. The spiked recovery of Cu(II) and L-Cys in lake water was verified the feasibility of the developed optosensors with a recovery of 98.2–101.6 % and 103.3–121.6 %. This method for detecting Cu(II) and L-Cys can not only recognize metal ions from actual samples, but also effectively protect CDs from quenching and restore fluorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetric
Biomass carbon dots
Cu(II)
Smartphone
Optosensing
L-cysteine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the RCDs, BCDs and red-to-blue colorimetric probe
Fluorescence measurements of Cu2+ and L-Cys based on red-to-blue colorimetric probe
Selectivity and interference of red-to-blue colorimetric probe
The construction and recognition mechanism of red-to-blue colorimetric probe
Fluorescence sensing of the red-to-blue colorimetric probe by Cu2+ and L-Cys
Competitiveness and interference of red-to-blue colorimetric probe
Fluorescence stability of red-to-blue colorimetric probe
Smartphone-integrated optosensing platform for real-time detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272413), the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1602300), and the Supported by the Research Foundation for Youth Scholars of Beijing Technology and Business University (QNJJ2021-11).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of red-to-blue colorimetric probe (B-RCDs, black), the probe + Cu2+ (red), and the probe + Cu2+ + L-Cys (blue). (B) Transient fluorescence spectrum of red-to-blue colorimetric probe (black), the probe + Cu2+ (red), and the probe + Cu2+ + L-Cys (blue).

(A) TEM image of red-to-blue colorimetric probe with a scale bar of 50 nm (inset: size distribution of red-to-blue colorimetric probe). (B) SEM image of red-to-blue colorimetric probe. (C) EDS spectrum of red-to-blue colorimetric probe. (D-G) Element mapping images of red-to-blue colorimetric probe.

(A) XPS spectrum of red-to-blue colorimetric probe. High-resolution XPS peaks of red-to-blue colorimetric probe: (B) C1s; (C) N1s; (D) O1s; (E) Si 2p, (F) FT-IR spectrum of red-to-blue colorimetric probe.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of the red-to-blue colorimetric probe with different concentrations of Cu2+. (B) Linear correlation between F 680/F 460 of the probe and LC concentration (Insert: under the irradiation of UV light box, the color changes in the mixed system). (C) Linear relationship in the Cu2+ concentration range from 0.1 μM to 2 μM. (D) Linear relationship of Cu2+ with the concentration between 5 μM and 200 μM.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the red-to-blue colorimetric probe-Cu2+ system to different concentrations of L-Cys. (B) Linear correlation between F 680/F 460 of the probe and the LC concentration (Insert: Linear relationship of red-to-blue colorimetric probe-Cu2+ system to different concentrations of L-Cys between 0.06 μM and 100 μM).

(A) Selectivity of common metal ions (100 μM) on fluorescence intensity of red-to-blue colorimetric probe. (B) Influences of effect of other metal ions with red-to-blue colorimetric probe-Cu2+ system. (C) Selectivity of common amino acids and thiol-active species (100 μM) on fluorescence intensity of red-to-blue colorimetric probe-Cu2+ system.

(A) The process of detecting by smartphone-based fluorescence sensing platform. (B) Linear relationship between the ratio of red to blue (R/B) and Cu2+ concentrations between 0.1 μM to 2 μM. (C) Linear relationship between the ratio of red to blue (R/B) and Cu2+ concentrations between 5 μM to 200 μM. (D) Linear relationship between the ratio of red to blue (R/B) and L-Cys concentrations between 0.06 μM to 100 μM.
The synthesis process and fluorescence answer-response of red-to-blue colorimetric probe.
Detection of Cu(II) and L-Cys in lake water samples.
Smartphone-assisted detection in lake water by using ratiometric red-to-blue colorimetric probe.

head.ce:title: Red-to-blue colorimetric probe based on biomass carbon dots for smartphone-integrated optosensing of Cu(II) and L-cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qingdao Grain and Oils Quality Inspection and Military Grain and Oils Supply Center, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qingdao Grain and Oils Quality Inspection and Military Grain and Oils Supply Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qingdao Grain and Oils Quality Inspection and Military Grain and Oils Supply Center, Qingdao 266042, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We constructed a smartphone-integrated optosensor with inexpensive, reversible, environmental friendly, and rapid adsorption to detect Cu(II) and L-cysteine (L-Cys). The key part of this study was to prepare a red-to-blue colorimetric probe from herbaceous andrographis paniculata using one-pot polymerization at room temperature. When Cu(II) existed, the red fluorescence on the surface of the core–shell probe was quenched, while the blue fluorescence of the core did not respond, because the colorimetric probe interacted with the Cu(II) on the surface of red CDs. After L-Cys added, it interacted with the Cu(II) to strip it from the surface of red CDs, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence response. Under optimal conditions, the detection limits of this method for Cu(II) and L-Cys were 71 nM and 12 nM, respectively. Further, the red-to-blue colorimetric probe was integrated into smartphone with a software application to convert fluorescent color images into specific red (R), green (G), and blue (B) values. The spiked recovery of Cu(II) and L-Cys in lake water was verified the feasibility of the developed optosensors with a recovery of 98.2–101.6 % and 103.3–121.6 %. This method for detecting Cu(II) and L-Cys can not only recognize metal ions from actual samples, but also effectively protect CDs from quenching and restore fluorescence.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are spherical nanoparticles with sizes about 3–5 nm, as a novel 0-dimensional carbon nanomaterial family, with the advantages of stable photoluminescence, polychromatic fluorescence, wide excitation spectrum, surface tunable function and water solubility [1,2]. CDs have attracted a great deal of attention since its accidental discovery. Compared with organic dyes or traditional semiconductor quantum dots, CDs exhibit low toxicity, low photobleaching, good biocompatibility and environmental friendliness [3,4]. Another unparalleled advantage of quantum dots is the rich source of CDs, such as graphite [5], glucose [6], watermelon [7], borassus flabellifer [8], citric acid [9], etc. Especially it can be synthesized from rich and non-toxic materials such as various resource wastes of biomass, it is also considered as a candidate to replace traditional luminescence semiconductor, especially in the biosensing field. Meanwhile, most of them exhibit blue luminescence, and only a few research reports on long-wavelength red luminescence of CDs. Red luminescence CDs have attracted extensive attention due to their attractive advantages in biosensor such as avoidable interference of autofluorescence and background.

In recent years, biosensor have attracted more and more attention because they can distinguish various targets with high classification accuracy, including optosensor and colorimetric sensor [10–12]. Although the optosensor has high sensitivity and strong resolution, there are still problems in photobleaching of various luminescent materials as sensing elements [13]. The colorimetric sensor has the advantages of high sensitive and visual response, compared with optosensor [14,15]. The output terminals of optosensors include multifunctional enzyme-labeled instrument, fluorescence spectrophotometer, electrochemiluminescence, Raman spectroscopy, near infrared spectroscopy, microfluidic equipment, etc [16–20]. These methods are fast, automatic and small portable, which can effectively make up for the defects of laboratory testing methods. However, smartphones are the most popular and easy-to-use digital intelligent output terminals. Because smartphones have excellent built-in devices such as high-resolution digital cameras and advanced multi-core processors, which can be expanded by adding applications to develop portable and easy-to-use “all-in-one devices” for sensing [21].

During these years, the research of biological cations and thiol-containing amino acids has attracted great attention because they play a key role in biological processes. L-cysteine (L-Cys) is an important biological thiol-containing amino acid, which plays key roles in the maintenance of physiological redox homeostasis [22] and is widely used in the biopharmaceutical, animal feed, cosmetics industries and food industry [23]. Abnormal L-Cys levels in the life system are associated with many diseases, including stunting, brain edema, liver injury, and Alzheimer's disease [24,25]. L-Cys plays a great role in the formation of a variety of proteins, maintains intracellular redox homeostasis and metabolism, and combines with transition metals (including argentum, cadmium, copper and iron), with a strong binding force [26–29]. There are many methods for L-Cys detection, typical optosensor is widely used due to its high sensitivity, low detection limit and cost [30,31]. However, the main problem in the L-Cys detection is the interference of other thiol-based molecules, such as homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH), because they have similar structures for L-Cys, including sulfhydryl, carboxyl and amino groups [32]. So, it is still challenging to distinguish L-Cys from Hcy and GSH with a simple small molecule fluorescent probe. In addition, the third most abundant transition metal ions of Cu(II) in the human body after iron and zinc, which is important for electron transfer processes in biological reactions. Ion balance is crucial, if lack of Cu(II) in the body will affect enzyme activity and inhibit cell metabolism, while excess Cu(II) will lead to gastrointestinal disorders and liver and kidney damage [33–35].

In the study, we prepared a red-to-blue colorimetric probe from herbaceous andrographis paniculata using one-pot polymerization at room temperature. Red and blue CDs were prepared from andrographis paniculata aqueous solutions with different concentrations to form core–shell embedded nanostructures. By modifying aminosilane on the surface of CDs, it interacted with metal cations, resulting in the quenching of fluorescent signals. When L-Cys existed, it interacted with the copper ion to strip the Cu(II) from the surface of CDs, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence response. The red-to-blue colorimetric probe was integrated into smartphone for visual optosensing of L-Cys in actual samples with a software application of Colorpicker, which can convert fluorescent color images into specific red (R), green (G), and blue (B) values to perform signal recognition and analysis. This method for L-Cys detection could not only effectively protect CDs from quenching, but also reverse quenching and restore fluorescence (turn-on), because it could remove copper ions from the surface of CDs.

Andrographis paniculata was purchased in the local supermarket. Ammonia was obtained from Sinopharm Group Co., ltd. (Beijing, China). Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98 %) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxy silane (APTES, 98 %) were from J&K Science Co., ltd. (Beijing, China). Ethanol, acetonitrile, and methanol were supplied by Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (Tianjin, China).. 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide (C16mimBr) was purchased from McLean Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water used in all experiments was obtained from the school laboratory. CoCl2, ZnCl2, NiCl2, CdCl2, Ba(NO3)2, AgCl, CaCl2, CuSO4, FeSO4·6H2O, NaCl, MgCl2 were all from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. MnCl2, FeCl3·6H2O and AlCl3·6H2O were obtained from Aladdin. These 15 amino acids are from Alfa Aesar (Tianjin) Chemical Co., ltd. Lake samples were taken from Yuyuantan Park.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the RCDs, BCDs and red-to-blue colorimetric probe
ce:section.ce:para: Red CDs (RCDs) and blue CDs (BCDs) were synthesized by using two different solvent extraction methods for andrographis paniculata. First, in the synthesis step of RCDs, 10 g of andrographis paniculata was weighed and placed in a mortar, 20 mL of ethanol and 1 mL of deionized water were added for grinding. The juice was extracted, centrifuged to remove impurities, and then stored in a high-pressure reactor for heating at 180 °C within 4 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the mixture was filtered through a membrane (0.22 μm) to completely remove leaves and unreacted impurities. Finally, the collected solution was pale pink fluorescent and stored at 4 °C for further use. The preparation process of BCDs was similar as RCDs, except the amount of andrographis paniculata was 0.3 mg and the solvent was 30 mL of deionized water.

The synthesis steps of red-to-blue colorimetric probe were as follows: 300 µL of BCDs (15 mg/mL) aqueous solution and 200 µL of ammonia water were injected into 50 mL of water–ethanol solution (2:1, V:V) and stirred for 10 min. Then, 50 µL of TEOS was diluted to 10 mL with ethanol and added dropwise to the above solution, and stirring was continued for 5 h. Then 1 mL of RCDs (10 mg/mL) aqueous solution, 50 µL of APTES, 2 g of C16mimBr and 100 µL of TEOS were added, and the resulting mixture was stirred continuously in the dark for 12 h. After the reaction, the mixture was centrifuged to obtain a precipitate, which was washed with acetonitrile: methanol (2:1, V:V), and dried to obtain red-to-blue colorimetric probe.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging was achieved using an FEI Talos F200X electron microscope (FEI, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) imaging was taken using a SU8020 microscope (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in the range of 400 to 4000 cm were recorded on a Nicolet IS10 FT-IR spectrometer-1 (Nichols Instruments, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using an ESCALAB 250xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was performed with a NETZSCH STA 449 F5 analyzer (Netzsch, Germany). Fluorescence lifetimes were recorded with an FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). All fluorescence spectra were measured by using a Synergy H1 full-featured microplate reader (Biotek, Winooski, VT, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence measurements of Cu2+ and L-Cys based on red-to-blue colorimetric probe
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a certain amount of red-to-blue colorimetric probe was dissolved into deionized water with a concentration of 1 mg/mL. In order to study the method for detecting Cu2+, the dynamic adsorption experiment of Cu2+ was firstly studied. The fluorescence intensity was detected in a microplate reader at 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, 15, 17.5, 20, 22.5, 25, 27.5 and 30 min, respectively. For the static adsorption experiments of Cu2+, 1 mg/mL of red-to-blue colorimetric probe were mixed with 100 μL of Cu2+ solutions with different concentrations between 0.5 and 200 μM in a 96-well plate for recording the fluorescence intensity. To ensure the accuracy and reproducibility of the experiments, all fluorescence intensity measurements were performed under the same conditions with three repeated experiments.

Regarding the experiment to detect L-Cys, the dynamic adsorption experiment was also carried out first, and Cu2+ at a concentration of 100 μM was added from the red-to-blue colorimetric probe (1 mg/mL) to form a mixture. And then L-Cys was added on the mix-system for recording the fluorescence intensity within 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 12.5, 15, 17.5, 20, 22.5, 25, 27.5 and 30 min. After that, the static adsorption experiment of L-Cys was ranged from 0.06 to 400 μM, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at 400 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference of red-to-blue colorimetric probe
ce:section.ce:para: To study the selectivity and the interference of red-to-blue colorimetric probe for Cu2+ detection, other metal ions of Co2+、Mn2+、Ba2+、Ag+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Cd2+ were used in the study. The Cu2+ (100 μL, 100 μM) and the same concentration of other metal ions were react with 100 μL of red-to-blue colorimetric probe (1 mg/mL) to a 96-well plate, respectively. The fluorescence intensity was detected. For interference experiments of other ions, 1, 2 and 5 times of interfering ions were added in the solution of Cu2+, respectively, and the fluorescence intensity were recorded. To study amino acid selectivity, 14 amino acids including Arg, Val, Leu, Try, Pro, Tyr, Hcy, Iso, Phe, Met, Asp, Thr, Lys, His, and mercaptoethanol were used in the selectivity analysis of L-Cys. For the red-to-blue colorimetric probe/Cu2+ system, 100 μL of the interfering substances and L-Cys were added in the mixture system for recording the fluorescence intensity under excitation of 400 nm.

For the stability experiments, the fluorescence intensity of red-to-blue colorimetric probe at different pH was mainly studied from 5.5 to 8.0, and the change in fluorescence intensity was recorded under continuous excitation by a UV lamp with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The fluorescence intensities of red-to-blue colorimetric probe in high concentration salt solution and at room temperature within 30 days were further studied.

The red-to-blue colorimetric probe was mixed with different concentrations of Cu2+, and the reaction solution of each concentration was pipetted into a centrifuge tube. The centrifuge tubes were put into a portable UV light box with a UV lamp of 365 nm. The color change of the red-to-blue colorimetric probe in the centrifuge tube was recorded by a smartphone. The digital image was analyzed by the color recognition program (Colorpicker) installed by the smartphone, and RGB (R: red, G: green, B: blue) data was collected.

In order to investigate the feasibility of the developed method as a Cu2+ and L-Cys responsive fluorescence sensor, lake water from Yuyuantan Park, Beijing were used as the actual samples. These samples were filtered using a 0.22 μm filter to remove insoluble impurities. Different concentrations of 5, 10, 20 μM of Cu2+ and L-Cys were added to the water samples, respectively, and the spiked recovery was calculated using the standard addition method based on red-to-blue colorimetric nanoprobe for Cu2+ and L-Cys in the actual samples of lake water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The construction and recognition mechanism of red-to-blue colorimetric probe
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process and fluorescence answer-response of red-to-blue colorimetric probe was shown in Scheme 1 . First, BCDs and RCDs were obtained by the traditional one-step hydrothermal method, and then red-to-blue colorimetric probe was synthetized by coating RCDs on the surface of BCDs with one-pot room temperature reverse microemulsion polymerization. The TEOS with a thin silicon shell was deposited on a BCDs core. The RCDs were coupled on the surface of BCDs by adding C16mimBr, TEOS and APTES and performing co-hydrolysis and condensation reactions. The obtained red-to-blue colorimetric probe exhibited two emission wavelengths at 460 and 680 nm, correspond to blue and red fluorescence imaging, respectively (Fig. S1). Cu2+ could selectively interact with red-to-blue colorimetric probe because of the presence of a large number of amino groups on the surface of the nanoparticles. The nanomaterials combined to form copper complexes, resulting in quenching of the red fluorescence, while the blue fluorescence of the BCDs core remained, enabling a ratiometric fluorescence response to Cu2+. In addition, after the addition of L-Cys, the chelation bond of the thiol groups in L-Cys was stronger force with Cu2+ than that of red-to-blue colorimetric probe, therefore, Cu2+ on the surface of the probe was gradually removed by the L-Cys addition. This process resulted in the recovery of the red fluorescence of the probe. From the perspective of energy saving and emission reduction, the fluorescent probe constructed had good reusability in theory.

Generally, the fluorescence answer-response mechanism of red-to-blue colorimetric probe for metal ions could be divided into static quenching, dynamic quenching, resonance energy transfer, light-induced electron transfer, and so on. Inorder to find out the answer-response mechanism, we conducuted a series of experiments. As shown in the UV absorption spectrum in Fig. 1 A, before and after the Cu2+ and L-Cys addition, the position of the UV characteristic absorption wavelength of red-to-blue colorimetric probe did not change significantly. It could be seen from Fig. 1B that the decay lifetime of red-to-blue colorimetric probe was almost unchanged after adding Cu2+ and L-Cys, indicating that static quenching was the corresponding mechanism of fluorescence quenching for Cu2+. The fluorescence spectra of red-to-blue colorimetric probe at different excitation wavelengths were shown in Fig. S2. When excited in the range of 300–400 nm, the emission red-shifted and the fluorescence intensity decreased with further increase of the excitation wavelength. Under the excitation of 400 nm, the red-to-blue colorimetric probe exhibited the strongest fluorescence intensity, therefore, 400 nm was chose as the optimal excitation wavelength. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was a very important factor for optosensing probe, the QYs of BCDs and RCDs were 18 % and 44 % by calculated with the reference substance of quinine sulfate and rhodamine 6G, respectively (Table S1). RCDs had excellent fluorescence response performance, so they could more sensitively contact with the target as the shell. When the targets were adding, the changes in fluorescence intensity of RCDs were evident. While the fluorescence performance of BCDs was slightly lower than RCDs. In addition, it was wrapped in a silica gel shell to avoid its contact with the targets and protect the fluorescence performance of BCDs from damage. The red-to-blue colorimetric probe could identify the target accurately and sensitively. The QY of red-to-blue colorimetric probe was also calculated by the reference substance of rhodamine 6G, it was 28.7 % as shown in Table S1.

The morphological structures of RCDs, BCDs and red-to-blue colorimetric probe were analyzed by TEM. As shown in Fig. S3A and B, the particles of RCDs and BCDs were almost spherical and well dispersed, with average diameters of 1.8 nm and 2.3 nm, respectively (insert: size distribution). After the RCDs and BCDs were constructed to red-to-blue colorimetric probe, the result of TEM (Fig. 2
A) showed that a significant core–shell structure could be clearly observed with a significantly enlarged diameter to 170 nm (Insert Fig. 2A). The red-to-blue colorimetric probe was generally spherical and uniformly distributed by SEM (Fig. 2B). These results indicated that the shell layers were successfully coated on the surfaces of BCDs, and the red-to-blue colorimetric probe had been successfully prepared. EDS test could show the content and distribution of C, N, O and Si in red-to-blue colorimetric probe, as shown in Fig. 2C. The element content was C: 23.7 %, N: 4.08 %, O: 49.84 %, Si: 22.39 %, where the presence of Si further confirmed the formation of the silicon dioxide layer. In addition, the distribution of C, N, O, and Si was evaluated by elemental mapping analysis (Fig. 2D-G), which was consistent with the EDS results.

The chemical bonds and functional groups in red-to-blue colorimetric probe could be measured using XPS and FT-IR. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 3A) showed that the binding energy peaks of C(1 s), N(1 s), O(1 s) and Si(2p) were 284.9, 339.6 and 103.1 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3B, the three sub-peaks on the C(1 s) peak were 287.7 eV (CC/CC), 286.0 eV (CO) and 288.5 eV (CO), respectively. In Fig. 3C, the N(1 s) spectrum had two peaks, NH2 at 399.3 eV and NHC at 401.3 eV. It showed that the amino groups were exposed outside the red-to-blue colorimetric probe and could interact with metal ions. In addition, there were three sub-peaks at 531.05, 532.63 and 533.16 eV in Fig. 3D, which was caused by CO, SiO and CO groups, respectively. Finally, the spectrum of Si (2p) showed a peak at 103.1 eV in Fig. 3E, which was attributed to the SiO group, thus indicating that there was a silicon layer outside the red-to-blue colorimetric probe. As shown in Fig. S4 A, the strong bands at 3425 cm−1 correspond to the NH characteristic vibrational bands on the surface of BCDs. The weak absorption peak at 1658 cm−1 was the characteristic absorption band of CO. An obvious band at 1453 cm−1 was derived from the COO − stretching vibration. The band at 1151 cm−1 was attributed to CNO groups. A stretching vibration peak at 1453 cm−1 resulted from CO3 2− bands. As for RCDs, there were obvious broad peaks in the range of 3201–3410 cm−1, indicating the presence of amino and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface. The characteristic absorption peak at 2926 cm−1 was mainly from the stretching vibration of CH, the weak absorption peak at 1668 cm−1 was the typical absorption band of CO. As for the red-to-blue colorimetric probe, there were obvious broad peaks in the range of 3432 cm−1, indicating the presence of NH bands on the surface (Fig. 3F). The characteristic absorption peak at 2920 cm−1 was mainly from the stretching vibration of CH, the weak absorption peak at 1626 cm−1 was mainly due to the stretching vibration of CO, the obvious absorption peak at 1059 cm−1 was the typical absorption of SiOSi, while 782 cm−1 and 458 cm−1 were the characteristic absorptions of SiO [36]. At the same time, the FT-IR results were consistent with the XPS data results, which also proved that the silicon-containing shell was wrapped outside the red-to-blue colorimetric probe, and the presence of amino groups ensured the fluorescence response of the probe to Cu2+. The successfully prepared red-to-blue colorimetric probe was further illustrated. Fig. S4B was the pyrolysis curve of the red-to-blue colorimetric probe. In the range of 0–700 °C, the TG curve showed a slow downward trend with a weight loss of about 17 %, indicating that the material was relatively stable. It could be seen from the differential TG and DTG curves that the weight loss of the nanomaterials increased rapidly at about 723 °C. This indicated that the structure of nanomaterials was disintegrated, the macromolecular structure was destroyed, the functional groups were cleaved, and the molecular chain was broken. As the temperature continued to increase, the TG and DTG curves remained basically unchanged, and the weight loss rate remained basically stable, indicating that the pyrolysis was completed at this time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of the red-to-blue colorimetric probe by Cu2+ and L-Cys
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to study the optimal adsorption time for the detection of Cu2+ by red-to-blue colorimetric probe, as shown in Fig. S5A, the optimal adsorption time of Cu2+ was 10 min. Fig. S5B showed the optimal adsorption time after adding L-Cys to the reaction system for 5 min. The potential of red-to-blue colorimetric probe to detect Cu2+ was explored under the optimal adsorption time. In Fig. 4A, we could see that the fluorescence intensity at 680 nm was gradually quenched as the Cu2+ concentration increased from 0.1 μM to 200 μM, while the fluorescence intensity at 460 nm remained basically unchanged. The relationship between Cu2+ concentration and fluorescence intensity ratio of F 680/F 460 was shown in Fig. 4B. The F 680/F 460 gradually decreased with the increase of Cu2+ concentration. Under the irradiation of the UV light box, the color change of the mixed system could be clearly observed, which gradually quenched from pink to blue. Therefore, according to the fitted Stern-Volmer linear equation:
(1)
F 680/F 460 = K sv[C] + 1
where F680 and F460 were the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 680 and 460 nm, respectively. Ksv was the binding constant, and Q was the concentration of the target.

There were two linear relationships in the Cu2+ concentration range of 0.1–200 μM, the linear equation was y = -0.319x + 2.549 in the linear range of 0.1–2 μM, with R2 of 0.983, while the linear equation was y = -0.00534x + 1.747 in the concentration range of 5–200 μM. Calculated based on the signal regulation ratio of 3δ/s, which δ signified the standard deviation of the blank signals and s was the slope of the calibration curve, the detection limit was 71 nM.

Fig. 5 A showed the effect of the concentration of L-Cys on the fluorescence intensity of the system. As the L-Cys concentration increased from 0.06 μM to 200 μM, the fluorescence response of the system gradually increased. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the red-to-blue colorimetric nanomaterial-Cu2+ system and the L-Cys concentration was shown in Fig. 5B. With the increase of L-Cys concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the system gradually increased. In the concentration range of 0.06–100 μM, there was a good linear relationship (shown insert Fig. 5B). Similarly, according to the fit equation (1), y = 0.00375x + 0.930, R2 = 0.967, calculated based on the signal voltage regulation ratio of 3δ/s, the detection limit was 12 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Competitiveness and interference of red-to-blue colorimetric probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the high selectivity of red-to-blue colorimetric probe for Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity ratios (F 680/F 460) of red-to-blue colorimetric probe and 13 metal ions were investigated under the same conditions. Compared with the fluorescence intensity (F 680/F 460) in the presence of 13 other metal ions and Cu2+, only Cu2+ could obviously quench the fluorescence of red-to-blue colorimetric probe (Fig. 6 A). It was shown in Fig. 6B, the ratio of F 680/F 460 did not change significantly in the presence of other ions. It indicated that red-to-blue colorimetric probe had better selectivity for Cu2+ than other metal ions.

Furthermore, considering that amino acids and other thiol-active species might interact with red-to-blue colorimetric probe and interfere with Cu2+ sensing, Arg, Val, Leu, Try, Pro, Tyr, Hcy, Iso, Phe, Met, Asp, Thr, Lys, His, L-Cys and mercaptoethanol were performed in the study. As shown in Fig. 6C, after adding the above substances to the red-to-blue colorimetric nanomaterial-Cu2+ reaction system, only L-Cys could significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity (F 680/F 460) of the system, while the addition of other substances, and the change in the fluorescence intensity was only a little. It indicated that the synthesized red-to-blue colorimetric probe showed higher selectivity for L-Cys than any other interfering substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability of red-to-blue colorimetric probe
ce:section.ce:para: This study also studied the stability of the red-to-blue colorimetric probe. First, the changes in fluorescence intensity within 30 days at room temperature were detected. As shown in Fig. S6A, the fluorescence intensity of the red-to-blue colorimetric probe basically did not change, indicating that it still had good stability after being placed at room temperature for 30 days. Fig. S6B showed that there was no significant difference in the relative fluorescence intensity when 365 nm UV light was continuously irradiated for 60 min, indicating that the red-to-blue colorimetric probe still had good fluorescence intensity after continuous UV light irradiation. In addition, Fig. S6C illustrated the fluorescence stability of red-to-blue colorimetric probe at different concentrations of high NaCl concentration, it could be seen that with the increase of NaCl concentration, the changes in fluorescence intensity was negligible, indicating that the probe could maintain good stability in high concentration salt ion solution. In Fig. S6D, red-to-blue colorimetric probe had good fluorescence stability in solutions of different pH from 5.5 to 8.0.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of the red-to-blue colorimetric probe, the detection ability of actual samples was very important. In the study, we used lake water as the actual samples for Cu2+ and L-Cys detection. As shown in Table 1 , the lake water samples did not contain the target substances. The spiked recovery for Cu2+ and L-Cys was further designed to verify the accuracy of the developed method. The operation steps were all carried out under the same conditions. The spiked recoveries of Cu2+ and L-Cys were from 104 % to 105.6 % and 85.1 %-114.4 %, and the RSD were less than 2.1 % and 1.57 %, respectively. The results showed that the red-to-blue colorimetric probe had a good recovery, and could use for the detection Cu2+ and L-Cys in environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-integrated optosensing platform for real-time detection

ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, we prepared a red-to-blue colorimetric probe based on core–shell biomass CDs from herbaceous andrographis paniculata using one-pot polymerization at room temperature. By introducing APTES on the surface of CDs, it interacted with Cu(II), resulting in the quenching of fluorescent signals. When L-Cys existed, they interacted with the Cu(II) to strip the Cu(II) from the surface of CDs, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence response. Under optimal conditions, the linear range of this colorimetric probe was 0.1–2 μM and 5–200 μM for Cu(II), and the detection limit was 71 nM and 95 nM by fluorescence spectrum and smartphone scanning, respectively. For L-Cys, the linear range of this colorimetric probe was 0.06–100 μM, and the detection limit was 12 nM and 50 nM by fluorescence spectrum and smartphone scanning, respectively. For the fluorescence spectrum, the satisfactory spiked recovery of 98.6–101.1 % and 97.6–103.4 % was obtained for Cu(II) and L-Cys in lake water. The spiked recovery was 98.2–101.6 % and 103.3–121.6 % for Cu(II) and L-Cys by smartphone-integrated optosensors. The high acceptance, simplicity and ease-of-use of smartphones all over the world had promoted the identification and detection of compounds. This method has the potential to be further popularized and applied in the future.

Dianwei Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Furui Zhang: Methodology, Validation. Shengnan Wang: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Sha Hu: Writing – original draft. Yonghong Liao: Conceptualization, Supervision. Fenghuan Wang: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Huilin Liu: Manuscript review & editing, Data analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32272413), the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1602300), and the Supported by the Research Foundation for Youth Scholars of Beijing Technology and Business University (QNJJ2021-11).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122285.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
